schools.summary: The men of Ephraim quarrelling with Jephthah and threatening to burn his house: 'and the men of Ephraim gathered themselves together, and went northward, and said unto Jephthah, Wherefore passedst thou over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didst not call us to go with thee? we will burn thine house upon thee with fire' — Ephraim, in envy, quarrelled with Jephthah for not calling them and threatened him (the and the men of Ephraim gathered themselves together, and went northward, and said unto Jephthah, Wherefore passedst thou over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didst not call us to go with thee?, the quarrel of Ephraim, the we will burn thine house upon thee with fire, the threat, the figure, the envy that quarrels with the victor for the honour it did not share, the truth that Ephraim, envious as before with Gideon, quarrelled with Jephthah, Judges 8:1, the figure of the pride and envy that begrudges another's success, the strife of jealousy, the quarrel of Ephraim, the threat of fire, vv.8:1); Ephraim complaining of not being called (the envy, Judges 8:1) — the complaint (not called); 'we will burn thine house upon thee with fire' (the violent threat against Jephthah, the menace, the figure, the pride and envy that begrudges another's success, the providence, the quarrel, Jephthah's answer to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jephthah answering that he had called Ephraim and they did not help: 'and Jephthah said unto them, I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon; and when I called you, ye delivered me not out of their hands' — Jephthah answered that he had called them and they failed to come (the and Jephthah said unto them, I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon, the great strife, the and when I called you, ye delivered me not out of their hands, the call unanswered, the figure, the just defense against an unjust charge, the truth against the slander, the truth that Jephthah had indeed called Ephraim, who failed him, Judges 11:7, the figure of the faithful man defending himself against the envious charge, the defense of Jephthah, the call unanswered, vv.1); Jephthah recalling the great strife with Ammon (the peril) — the strife (with Ammon); 'when I called you, ye delivered me not out of their hands' (Ephraim's failure to answer the call, the charge refuted, Judges 11:7, the figure, the faithful man defending himself against the envious charge, the providence, the defense, Jephthah's further answer to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jephthah recalling that he risked his life and God gave the victory: 'and when I saw that ye delivered me not, I put my life in my hands, and passed over against the children of Ammon, and the LORD delivered them into my hand: wherefore then are ye come up unto me this day, to fight against me?' — Jephthah recalled his peril and God's deliverance, asking why they now attacked him (the and when I saw that ye delivered me not, I put my life in my hands, and passed over against the children of Ammon, the life risked, the and the LORD delivered them into my hand: wherefore then are ye come up unto me this day, to fight against me?, God gave the victory, the figure, the man who ventured his life and ascribed the victory to God, met now with envy, the truth that Jephthah risked his life and God gave the victory, yet Ephraim attacked him, 1 Samuel 19:5, the figure of the deliverer who owns God's hand, set against the envy that fights him, the appeal of Jephthah, the victory ascribed to God, vv.2); Jephthah venturing his life against Ammon (the peril, 1 Samuel 19:5) — the venture (life in my hands); 'the LORD delivered them into my hand: wherefore then are ye come up unto me this day, to fight against me?' (God's gift of victory and the unjust attack, the appeal, the figure, the deliverer who owns God's hand, set against envy, the providence, the appeal, Jephthah gathering Gilead to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jephthah gathering Gilead and smiting Ephraim, who had taunted them: 'then Jephthah gathered together all the men of Gilead, and fought with Ephraim: and the men of Gilead smote Ephraim, because they said, Ye Gileadites are fugitives of Ephraim among the Ephraimites, and among the Manassites' — Gilead smote Ephraim, provoked by their taunt (the then Jephthah gathered together all the men of Gilead, and fought with Ephraim: and the men of Gilead smote Ephraim, Gilead smote Ephraim, the because they said, Ye Gileadites are fugitives of Ephraim among the Ephraimites, and among the Manassites, the taunt, the figure, the civil war kindled by envy and insult, brother against brother, the truth that Ephraim's taunt provoked the battle, Gilead smiting them, Judges 8:1, the figure of the strife of brethren, envy ending in bloodshed, the battle with Ephraim, the taunt avenged, vv.1); Gilead smiting Ephraim (the battle, Judges 8:1) — the battle (Gilead smote Ephraim); 'Ye Gileadites are fugitives of Ephraim' (the insult that provoked the war, the taunt, the figure, the strife of brethren, envy ending in bloodshed, the providence, the battle, the fords of Jordan taken to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gilead taking the fords of Jordan and challenging each fugitive: 'and the Gileadites took the passages of Jordan before the Ephraimites: and it was so, that when those Ephraimites which were escaped said, Let me go over; that the men of Gilead said unto him, Art thou an Ephraimite? schools.summary: The shibboleth test, the word the Ephraimites could not pronounce, which betrayed them to their death: 'then said they unto him, Say now Shibboleth: and he said Sibboleth: for he could not frame to pronounce it right. Then they took him, and slew him at the passages of Jordan: and there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand' — the dialect that betrayed — 'then said they unto him, Say now Shibboleth: and he said Sibboleth: for he could not frame to pronounce it right. Then they took him, and slew him': the Ephraimites, unable to sound the sh, were exposed by a single syllable (the witnesses noting the significance — the simple yet infallible test by which the men of Gilead unmasked the disguised Ephraimites, the slight difference of dialect, the inability to frame the sh sound, betraying the fugitive who denied his tribe, a small and seemingly trifling mark that yet discovered with certainty what a man was and sealed his fate, the one word that no denial could counterfeit, the speech that betrayed the heart it sought to hide, forty-two thousand falling by a syllable, the lesson — that a man's true character and origin are betrayed by small and unstudied things, the slight token that the disguise overlooks revealing what he is, 'thy speech bewrayeth thee,' Matthew 26:73, the truth that there is no hiding from the test God appoints, the false profession unmasked by what it cannot counterfeit, the heart known by its accent, 'by thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned,' Matthew 12:37, the figure — the searching trial that separates the true from the false, fulfilled in the day when no disguise will pass and the secret of every heart is laid bare, 'the LORD seeth not as man seeth... the LORD looketh on the heart,' 1 Samuel 16:7, the truth that the smallest thing may be the test of the greatest issue, life or death hanging on a syllable, the figure of the small mark that infallibly discovers what a man is, the test no disguise can pass, the call to be in truth what we profess, lest a small thing betray us, the warning that the false profession will be unmasked at the last, the figure of the small mark that infallibly discovers what a man is, the test that no disguise can pass); the Ephraimites unable to frame the sh sound, betrayed by a single syllable, the slight difference of dialect unmasking the fugitive who denied his tribe (the speech that betrays, Matthew 26:73) — the test (Shibboleth/Sibboleth); 'then they took him, and slew him at the passages of Jordan: and there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand' (the Ephraimites slain at the fords, forty-two thousand falling by the test, the false denial unmasked by what it could not counterfeit, the heart known by its accent, Matthew 12:37) — the figure (the searching trial that separates the true from the false, the providence, the test, the death of Jephthah to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the shibboleth test signify?

sides.position: The small mark that infallibly discovers what a man is, the test no disguise can pass - the slight difference of dialect betraying the disguised Ephraimite who denied his tribe, a man's true character betrayed by small unstudied things ('thy speech bewrayeth thee,' Matthew 26:73), no hiding from the test God appoints (Matthew 12:37), the searching trial that lays bare the heart (1 Samuel 16:7)

themes: Then said they unto him, Say now Shibboleth: and he said Sibboleth: for he could not frame to pronounce it right. Then they took him, and slew him at the passages of Jordan: and there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand
The shibboleth test, the word the Ephraimites could not pronounce, which betrayed them to their death (the crux of the small mark that infallibly discovers what a man is)

the simple yet infallible test by which Gilead unmasked the disguised Ephraimites, the slight difference of dialect betraying the fugitive who denied his tribe, a small mark that discovered with certainty what a man was
a man's true character betrayed by small and unstudied things, no hiding from the test God appoints, the searching trial that separates true from false (Matthew 26:73; Matthew 12:37; 1 Samuel 16:7)
